experiment: M_EXT — Flask External Validation of Functional Proximity Law
description: First external-party validation of the Functional Proximity Law on data NOT designed by the IRDME author. Dataset: Flask web framework (github.com/pallets/flask), 14 core Python modules, 3 layers extracted from real source code and git history. Layers: imports (static dependency), structural_coupling (shared import targets), co_change (behavioral — files changed together in git commits). The law predicts: r(imports <-> structural_coupling) > r(imports <-> co_change) because imports and structural_coupling both encode static architecture (same relational regime) while co_change encodes developer work patterns (different relational regime). This is the same pattern confirmed in v1.2 on the IRDME codebase itself (r=0.9411 vs r=-0.3037). If confirmed on Flask, external validity of the law is established.

hypotheses.statement: Hub persistence between imports and structural_coupling is higher than between imports and co_change — the structurally similar pair (same relational regime: static architecture) has higher r than the dissimilar pair (different regime: behavioral coupling)
r(imports <-> structural_coupling) >= 0.50 — structural coupling is derived from the import topology so strong correlation is expected

r(imports <-> co_change) < r(imports <-> structural_coupling) — behavioral coupling diverges from static import structure because developer work patterns are shaped by feature proximity, not module topology alone
app is the top hub in the imports layer — Flask's application class is the central dependency target across modules
